student_response:
Indigenous societies adapted to European colonists in North America from 1500 to 1754 through trade alliances, selective conversion, diplomatic federation, and warfare, and those strategies show both continuity with older Indigenous politics and sharp change as colonial farms, missions, and slave economies spread. Before English towns dominated the Atlantic coast, Native nations already negotiated with rivals, moved goods across long distances, and relocated villages after conflict or poor harvests. When Spanish, French, and English newcomers arrived, the first Indigenous responses often looked like familiar bargaining rather than sudden collapse.

In the southwest and Florida, Spanish missions and presidios pressed Indigenous people into labor drafts and baptism schedules, yet many communities kept kinship obligations and seasonal farming while taking metal tools, livestock, and later horses. Horses remade hunting and mobility on the plains over time, which is real change, but the deeper habit of folding new goods into older economies stayed continuous. French traders along the St. Lawrence and Great Lakes depended on Algonquian, Huron, and later other partners for furs. Mixed marriages and gift diplomacy continued because they served both sides, even while epidemic disease cut populations and forced survivors to rebuild villages or join stronger neighbors. Adaptation here meant staying inside the fur trade network rather than abandoning it.

English settlement after Jamestown in 1607 created denser pressure on coastal peoples. Powhatan leaders first tried controlled exchange of corn and copper for English manufactures, then resisted when tobacco planters seized fields. Headright grants accelerated land claims, and Indigenous strategy swung between temporary peace, raids, and attempts to exploit English factional fights. Further north, Wampanoag and neighboring nations dealt with Plymouth and Massachusetts settlers who brought crowded townscapes, English livestock, and missionary ambition. Early agreements and gift exchanges looked like continuity with older diplomacy, yet trampling of cornfields and expanding fences made conflict harder to contain.

Metacom, called King Philip by the English, led a major war in the 1670s when land loss and cultural pressure peaked. King Philip's War burned towns on both sides and afterward many survivors faced enslavement, exile to the west, or tighter surveillance on reserved lands. That conflict marks a turning point: open military coalitions became riskier in New England, so later adaptation leaned toward petitions, temporary wartime alliances, and cultural persistence inside shrinking spaces. Continuity remained in efforts to protect language, ceremony, and hunting access wherever possible.

In the Chesapeake, Bacon's Rebellion tangled frontier settlers and Native communities as colonists blamed Indigenous groups for raids while leaders like Berkeley pursued uneven trade and punishment policies. Native responses included moving villages inland, seeking French or Spanish shelter, or negotiating tribute arrangements. Along the middle Atlantic, Lenape and other peoples sold or were pressured into selling tracts while trying to keep hunting corridors open. William Penn's early Quaker diplomacy offered a calmer negotiating window, yet speculation and migration still eroded Native title across decades.

Trade stayed a continuous adaptation tool across the whole period. Mercantilist rules and triangular Atlantic commerce meant colonists wanted furs, deerskins, and food surpluses, while Indigenous traders wanted guns, cloth, kettles, and iron. Guns changed hunting ranges and inter-tribal warfare, which is change, but using outsiders' goods for local status and survival was not new. Missionaries and established churches added spiritual pressure; some people converted fully, others borrowed Christian symbols while keeping older beliefs. Debates inside English churches, including Halfway Covenant questions about membership, mattered more to colonists than to Native theology, yet mission villages still became places where families weighed conversion against belonging.

By the early 1700s, colonial assemblies, slave codes, and Navigation Acts strengthened English control of coasts and rivers. Indigenous societies adapted by shifting into contested backcountries, allying with France during imperial rivalry, or specializing as go-betweens who interpreted languages and markets. The Iroquois Covenant Chain shows continuity of diplomatic federation used to manage Europeans rather than simply flee them. At the same time, disease and the Middle Passage driven plantation complex remade the map: as African slavery expanded in southern colonies, English demand for land intensified and Native farming communities faced more displacement from tobacco and rice frontiers.

Regional differences mattered. In New England after Metacom's defeat, some Native people lived in praying towns under missionary supervision while others moved toward French Catholic allies. In the southeast, Creek, Cherokee, and related nations played English and Spanish agents against each other, selling deerskins while trying to regulate settler trespass. Continuity appeared in clan leadership and consensus politics; change appeared when guns, debt, and land cessions altered who held influence inside those societies. Early Atlantic contact had already introduced European goods in the far north, but denser seventeenth-century settlements made adaptation daily and unavoidable.

Colonial politics such as the House of Burgesses or Dominion experiments mainly served English settlers, yet Indigenous peoples still had to read those power shifts because governors and assemblies set war and trade rules. When imperial wars flared, Native alliances could tip local balances, which is why French and English officials competed for Indigenous partners. That competition is continuity of diplomacy under new flags. By 1754, on the eve of a larger imperial war, Indigenous nations still acted as strategic players, but their coastal autonomy was far weaker than in 1500.

Overall, Indigenous adaptation never meant one single story of either total resistance or total assimilation. Continuity showed in diplomacy, selective trade, and cultural persistence under pressure. Change showed in catastrophic population loss, loss of coastal territory after wars, growing dependence on European markets and weapons, and the narrowing of independent military options. From first contact through the mid-eighteenth century, Native nations kept trying to survive on their own terms, yet colonial environments of tobacco, towns, missions, and imperial rivalry steadily closed older choices while forcing new ones.

European colonists came looking for land wealth and converts and Indigenous societies had to adapt and they adapted by fighting and also by trading and sometimes by moving. Continuity is that natives always tried to keep their communities alive. Change is that after big wars they held less coastal land. Spanish and french people tried converting natives and some natives took christian practices but still farmed in older seasonal ways. When Jamestown started the powhatan traded corn then later fought because settlers took fields for tobacco and that same pattern shows up in New England too. First agreements then conflict then more agreements that favor colonists. King Phillips war shows change because after that natives were weaker and more people got pushed inland or onto smaller reserved spaces. They adapted by moving west or allying with french traders for guns and protection. Trade is continuity because exchanging goods was already part of Native life. Guns cloth and alcohol are change because those goods altered hunting and diplomacy. Some tribes allied with colonists against rival tribes which is old diplomacy but with new partners and new weapons. Mission towns tried to make natives live like english farmers in tight lots. Some did for a while. Some left. Overall natives kept trying to survive and negotiate even while disease and settlers changed the map around them. Land conflict repeats over and over in different colonies. Adaptation looks like fighting then trading then relocating then trading again. Continuity of wanting independence from full English control. Change in how much power Native nations still had by 1754 compared with 1500. Bacon type frontier violence also pushed natives to relocate. Mercantilism made colonists hungrier for export crops so pressure on Native farmland grew. The pattern is uneven but clear enough. Indigenous societies adapted without disappearing yet their room to choose shrank. Same land fight keeps coming back in Virginia New England and the middle colonies. Continuity and change tangle together rather than arriving as separate clean chapters.

Between 1940 and 1980 civil rights organizing pushed government toward stronger equal rights laws, though courts and Congress sometimes moved slowly. Brown and later marches helped force school and voting reforms, while protests after Selma shaped the Voting Rights Act. Black activism also pressured Johnson-era laws on public accommodations, and related movements influenced later rights rulings. Gains were real, yet enforcement stayed uneven and backlash limited how far federal action went.

After English colonization began at Jamestown in 1607, British settlers adapted to American environments in regional ways that both continued English commercial habits and changed under new climates, soils, and Native neighbors. Continuity appeared in their desire for property, Protestant worship, representative local institutions, and trade inside mercantilist rules. Change appeared in the crops they chose, the labor systems they built, and how thoroughly they remade forests and rivers into Atlantic export zones.

In the Chesapeake, humid lowlands and a long growing season pushed settlers toward tobacco after early starvation crises. Learning corn horticulture and river geography from Powhatan peoples was an early adaptation, then planters used headrights to claim land and imported bound laborers. Over decades slave laws hardened racial slavery into the main plantation workforce. That shift was a major change from the early mix of indenture and Native exchange, even while export agriculture for English markets stayed continuous with mercantilism and the Navigation Acts. The House of Burgesses adapted English representation to a planter society that needed laws for land, labor, and defense.

New England offered rocky soils and colder winters, so settlers relied more on subsistence farming, fishing, timber, and coastal trade. Puritan towns clustered tightly for religion and mutual aid. The Mayflower Compact and later town meetings show political adaptation through covenants suited to compact communities rather than sprawling plantations. Relations with Native neighbors moved from early trade to King Philip's War, after which English control of interior land expanded and livestock farming spread. Continuity remained in English legal culture and church discipline; change showed in how much fisheries and timber shaped work calendars.

Middle colonies mixed grain agriculture, river commerce, and greater ethnic variety, while Carolina and West Indian-linked southern zones leaned into rice, indigo, and heavier reliance on enslaved labor. Across regions, triangular trade tied farms and ports to Atlantic markets in sugar, slaves, and manufactures. British settlers kept adapting by specializing: tobacco in the Chesapeake, wheat in the middle colonies, naval stores and rice farther south, fish and ships in New England. Environmental adaptation was never only weather. It also meant deciding which Native alliances or wars opened land, and which imperial policies rewarded staple exports.

Mercantilist expectations forced another kind of adaptation. Colonists needed goods that England would buy, so landscapes were reshaped around those staples even when local subsistence might have preferred different mixes. When tobacco prices fell, planters expanded acreage or diversified slightly rather than abandoning the staple model. In New England, thinner soils encouraged crafts and shipping that linked the region to Caribbean markets. Bacon's Rebellion revealed social strain on the Virginia frontier where land hunger and Native conflict collided, pushing colonial governments toward firmer Native policies and tighter elite control afterward.

Religious motives also shaped settlement patterns without erasing environmental pressure. Maryland's toleration experiment and New England church towns organized people differently, yet both still had to clear fields, store food for winter, and manage livestock in unfamiliar ecologies. Quaker Pennsylvania emphasized negotiated land purchases early on, which briefly reduced warfare compared with other frontiers, but migration still transformed forests into farms. By 1754 British America looked regionally different yet commercially oriented toward Britain. Continuity lay in English language, law, and market ambition. Change lay in African slavery's expansion, the decline of some early Native partnerships, and the creation of distinct colonial landscapes. Adaptation succeeded enough that the colonies were populous and productive, but that success rested on transforming environments and displacing many Indigenous communities who had shaped those same places first.

British settlers from 1607 to 1754 adapted to American environments by reshaping land use around regional climates while still chasing English commercial goals. Continuity lay in export-minded farming, Protestant community ideals, and reliance on English law; change lay in how tobacco, grains, fisheries, and enslaved labor replaced failed early dreams of quick treasure.

Jamestown survivors turned to tobacco after discovering the Chesapeake could support that staple, and headrights plus expanding slave labor remade the landscape into plantations tied to Navigation Act shipping. New Englanders faced poorer soils, so they mixed subsistence plots with fishing and timber and organized compact towns under covenants like the Mayflower Compact. Middle colonies grew wheat for Atlantic markets, showing another environmental path that relied less on a single luxury crop. Relations with Native peoples helped settlers at first through food and geographic knowledge, then often turned violent, as in King Philip's War, which opened more land for English farms even as it revealed the human cost of expansion. Mercantilism kept tying colonial produce to British ports, so adaptation meant fitting local environments into imperial trade rather than abandoning England. Carolinas later pushed rice and related staples with heavier slave systems, widening regional contrast. House of Burgesses style local government and slave codes show political adaptation following economic needs. By midcentury those regional differences were sharp, yet the broader pattern of commercial settlement continued, only now resting on African labor and reduced Native control of coastal plains. Continuity and change thus traveled together: English ambitions stayed, while the American environment forced new crops, new labor, and new frontiers of conflict.

British colonists adapted to there enviornment from 1607 to 1754 by growing crops that worked and fighting natives for land and also by building different kinds of towns. Continuity is they still wanted money trade and english style farms. Change is they used tobacco and slaves more in the south while New England did fishing and small farms. Jamestown almost failed then tobacco saved it and headrights brought more workers. New england soil was rocky so they did fishing timber and religous towns close together. Mayflower compact kind of shows them making rules in a new place when they felt far from england. They traded with natives first then wars like king philip war changed who controlled the woods and fields. Mercantilism made them send crops to britian. Navigation acts too which pushed export habits. Some places used slave laws later to make slavery permanent which is a big change in labor compared with early indenture. Bacon rebellion shows frontier people angry about land and natives and the government. Middle colonies grew grain and traded on rivers which is another adaptation. Overall settlers kept trying english life but the land forced different crops and more conflict and that pattern stays through 1754. Southern plantations look different than new england villages but both are adapting to local conditions while staying inside british trade. Triangle trade links them even when the farms look unlike each other. Adaptation is messy and uneven but it keeps happening. Continuity is english markets. Change is slavery and regional crops. That is basically the story.

U.S. foreign policy from 1890 to 1930 changed because the world changed and America reacted in stages that can be read as cause, comparison, and change over time. Cause first: industrial growth and Mahan style navy ideas pushed leaders to want bases and markets abroad. Then the Maine explosion and the Spanish American War brought islands under U.S. control and the Treaty of Paris settlement showed a new overseas empire. Comparison: before 1898 policy talked more about the hemisphere, after 1898 it also watched Asia with Open Door talk in China. Roosevelt Corollary and the Panama Canal made Caribbean intervention easier which is change over time from quieter earlier diplomacy. Dollar diplomacy tried loans instead of only troops. Anti imperialists complained but expansion still stuck. Then World War I forced another shift from neutrality to fighting in Europe. Afterward Wilson wanted the League but the Senate refused so the 1920s mixed limited treaties with pullback. The process is expansion, war, then cautious peace. Same pressure of markets and security keeps returning. Policy responds by getting more involved then stepping partly back when costs rise. That cycle is the main story of the period and it keeps repeating in different forms.

From 1820 to 1900 varied reform movements reacted to industrial growth by attacking the social problems that factories, cities, and market competition seemed to create. Continuity ran through moral perfection ideas rooted in the Second Great Awakening; change showed in how later reformers targeted wages, alcohol, slavery, and women's rights more directly as industrial life intensified.

Evangelical revivals taught that individuals could improve society, which fed temperance societies that blamed drink for disorder in growing towns and among wage workers. Abolitionists such as William Lloyd Garrison and Frederick Douglass linked free labor ideals to attacks on slavery, arguing that industrial free workers and enslaved labor could not share one republic forever without conflict. Women's rights activists at Seneca Falls used related language of equality when they issued the Declaration of Sentiments and challenged legal limits on property and voice. Utopian communities like Brook Farm and Oneida tried to escape competitive industry by living in cooperative settlements, while Dorothea Dix pushed asylum reforms as cities produced more visible poverty and illness. The American Colonization Society offered a different imperfect response by promoting removal rather than equality. Factory growth also made child labor and long hours more visible, so some reformers petitioned legislatures while others organized workers directly. By the late century, labor protests and early Progressive campaigns carried reform into factories themselves, demanding safer conditions and fairer pay. Industrial growth did not create one movement; it created many overlapping ones that kept translating religious and republican language into campaigns against the human costs of rapid economic change across the nineteenth century. Continuity was the belief that society could be perfected. Change was which industrial wound each group chose to treat first.

Reform movements from 1820 to 1900 reacted to industrial growth in kinda scattered ways that still share a moral tone. Second Great Awakening preaching made people think they should fix society not just themselves. Temperance groups said drinking ruined factory workers families and city order. Abolitionists like Garrison used newspapers to attack slavery while northern cities grew and free labor ideas spread. Seneca Falls women wanted legal rights too after seeing how reform work still shut them out. Some people built utopian places like Brook Farm to get away from competition. Dix tried to improve asylums for the mentally ill as urban poverty became harder to ignore. Later there were labor protests about wages and hours but the exact laws blur. Overall reformers protested alcohol slavery and unfair work because industry made cities crowded and money unequal. They used sermons petitions and newspapers more than one single national plan. Reaction was moral and religious at first then more about labor conditions by the end of the century. Continuity is the urge to improve people. Change is what problem they named as factories grew. The responses stay uneven and sometimes contradictory but they all start from industrial disruption.

Sectional rivalries shaped U.S. society from 1800 to 1848 by splitting the country into regions that lived differently and argued about slavery, land, and federal power. Continuity was the older north-south contrast over labor systems. Change was how cotton profits and western expansion made that contrast sharper in daily life and politics.

The cotton gin made short-staple cotton extremely profitable, so the Deep South plantation belt leaned harder into slavery and king cotton politics. That economic specialization differed from northeastern urbanization and mills that used free wage workers plus rising Irish and German immigration. Nativist anger and Know-Nothing style politics showed social tension inside the North too, but the louder rivalry remained section versus section. Nat Turner's rebellion terrified many southern whites and led to tighter slave codes, which made southern society more defensive and surveillance-heavy. Earlier Denmark Vesey fears in Charleston had already pointed the same direction. Meanwhile northern reformers talked free-soil ideas more loudly as new western lands opened and transportation linked farms to markets.

Missouri Compromise style bargains tried to keep Senate balance, yet every new territory restarted the fight about whether slavery would expand. Internal improvements and tariffs also felt sectional because many southern planters saw them as helping northern industry while taxing export staples. Society looked different in ordinary routines: market towns and mills in one region, plantations and enslaved labor in another, and a northwest farming belt pulling toward free-labor politics. Churches split arguments about slavery, newspapers amplified stereotypes, and migration streams sorted people into regions that reinforced those identities.

Transportation projects like canals and early railroads made regional economies more productive, which ironically sharpened rivalry because each section could imagine national policy serving its own growth. Southern leaders defended slavery as a social order, not only a labor system, while many northern voters increasingly called it a threat to free men's opportunities in the West. Those cultural claims shaped schools, pulpits, and party loyalty.

By 1848, after war with Mexico, the question of slavery in new southwestern lands made rivalries even hotter because victory created more territory to argue over. Sectionalism was not only speeches in Congress. It shaped what work looked like, who held wealth, how families talked about race, and which federal policies seemed fair. Some treaty details and exact vote counts stay fuzzy, but the social pattern is clear: rival regions built different societies, and those differences kept hardening through the 1840s instead of fading. Continuity of slavery conflict plus change from cotton and expansion together remade American life long before later disunion. The years to 1848 already show a society sorting itself into hostile sections even while still sharing one constitution.

Between 1800 and 1848 sectional rivalries shaped U.S. society because the North and South built different economies and then argued constantly about power. Continuity: slavery stays central in the South. Change: cotton and expansion make conflict worse and more public. The cotton gin locked the Deep South into king cotton and slave labor. Nat Turners rebellion made southern laws harsher and daily surveillance tighter. Meanwhile the Northeast urbanized and used immigrant Irish and German workers in mills so free labor culture grew. Nativism rose against those immigrants which is another social split but still inside a free-labor region. Sectional economic specialization meant tariffs banks and internal improvements felt unfair depending where you lived. Compromises over western land tried to keep peace yet every new state restarted rivalry about slave or free soil. Society divides into plantation life versus factory towns and that rivalry keeps repeating in politics churches and newspapers. Same argument again and again about whether new territory will be slave or free. By 1848 the social distance between sections is wider than in 1800 even if they still share one government. Rivalry shapes schooling migration and what people fear about the other region. It is repetitive because the same land-and-slavery problem returns each decade. Cotton wealth and northern industry keep feeding that loop. Continuity and change tangle: old slavery conflict, new intensity from expansion. The rivalry feels endless because expansion keeps reopening the same wound. The rivalry feels endless because expansion keeps reopening the same wound. The rivalry feels endless because expansion keeps reopening the same wound. The rivalry feels endless because expansion keeps reopening the same wound. The rivalry feels endless because expansion keeps reopening the same wound.

Sectional rivalries from 1800 to 1848 reshaped American society by tying everyday economic life to opposing visions of labor and expansion. Cotton gin profits entrenched plantation slavery across the Deep South, while northeastern urbanization and immigrant wage work strengthened a free-labor culture that viewed slavery as a rival social system rather than a distant peculiarity. Nat Turner's revolt hardened southern repression, and repeated fights over western territory turned political compromise into a temporary bandage rather than a lasting settlement. Immigration and nativism complicated northern politics without erasing the larger sectional split over slavery's future. By the late 1840s, churches, parties, and migration patterns already reflected two societies sharing one federal frame yet drifting toward collision as new southwestern lands raised the same question again. The social split was already structural before later secession made it military. The social split was already structural before later secession made it military.

From 1890 to 1930 U.S. foreign policy reacted to a changing world by moving from a mostly continental focus toward overseas power, then into world war, and finally into a confused peace that mixed treaties with reluctance. Continuity was interest in markets, naval security, and influence in the Western Hemisphere. Change was how far leaders were willing to seize islands, police neighbors, and debate permanent international organization.

In the 1890s writers like Alfred Thayer Mahan argued that sea power mattered for great nations, and industrial exporters wanted access to Asia and Latin America. When the USS Maine blew up in Havana harbor, the Spanish-American War followed quickly, and the Treaty of Paris left the United States holding the Philippines and Puerto Rico while claiming special influence over Cuba through later Platt-style controls. That war was a reaction to European empire competition, Cuban revolt, and sensational news that made neutrality harder to defend. Open Door diplomacy toward China tried to keep trade open without carving a formal American colony there. Anti-imperialists said overseas rule betrayed republican ideals, yet expansion still happened because strategic and commercial arguments won enough support in elections and Congress.

Theodore Roosevelt pushed a Corollary idea that the United States would intervene in the Caribbean if disorder threatened debt payments or stability, and the Panama Canal project showed how far policy would go to move warships and commerce between oceans. Hay negotiations and related canal diplomacy tied local politics in Panama to American naval plans. Dollar diplomacy under Taft preferred loans and investment as quieter tools, though marines still appeared when investments seemed endangered. These steps reacted to instability in neighboring countries and to rivalry with European lenders. Gentlemen's Agreement style diplomacy with Japan also showed leaders managing Pacific tension without full-scale war, especially over immigration friction on the West Coast.

World War I forced another major reaction. At first Wilson emphasized neutrality while American trade tied the country more tightly to the Allies. Unrestricted submarine warfare and the Zimmermann message then pulled the United States into the conflict. American troops and loans helped tip the balance, which made U.S. leaders feel entitled to shape the peace. After victory, Wilson promoted a League of Nations to manage future disputes and make the war's settlement more durable. The Senate rejected membership, so the 1920s mixed naval limitation talks and debt diplomacy with a public mood that disliked new European guarantees. Some treaty names blur together for a limited student, yet the arc remains expansion, wartime engagement, then partial retreat.

Economic motives never disappeared. Open markets in Asia, protected routes through Panama, and Caribbean interventions all answered commercial fear as much as ideology. At the same time, anti-imperialist critics kept asking whether ruling overseas populations fit the nation's self-image, which created uneven support for each new step. By 1930 the United States was not a classic isolationist spectator, but neither had it built a stable system of permanent alliances. Reaction, not a fixed doctrine, defined the era: each global shock produced a tool—war, corollary policing, loans, or league talk—and then domestic politics edited how far that tool would go. That unevenness is itself evidence of reactive policymaking across forty crowded years.

U.S. foreign policy from 1890 to 1930 reacted by getting more imperial then fighting in Europe then backing off into cautious deals. Continuity is wanting trade and naval strength. Change is formal empire and then the League fight. Mahan ideas about sea power made leaders build ships and look for coaling stations. The Maine sunk and the Spanish American War happened and the Treaty of Paris gave islands and new headaches in Asia. Open Door talk in China tried to protect markets without a full colony. Roosevelt Corollary and the Panama Canal show Caribbean control and faster naval movement between oceans. Dollar diplomacy tried money instead of only soldiers when Latin American debts looked unstable. Anti imperialists complained about ruling overseas peoples but policy still expanded because business and navy arguments were loud. Then World War I. Neutrality first then war after submarine attacks and the Zimmermann message pulled opinion. Wilson wanted a League of Nations and the Senate said no which left a messy peace and a lot of unfinished promises. 1920s quieter on alliances but not totally inactive because loans and naval talks continued. Rough organization because these pieces jump around instead of lining up neat. Overall reaction is more involvement overseas when crises hit then hesitation after a costly war. Same pattern of markets and security keeps showing up across decades. Gentlemen agreement style talks with Japan also show managing Pacific tension through diplomacy not only fleets. Platt controls over Cuba fit the same intervention habit after 1898. Policy never stays still from 1890 to 1930 and that instability is basically the point of the period. The story keeps circling back to ships markets and fear of falling behind europe. The story keeps circling back to ships markets and fear of falling behind europe. From 1890 to 1930 American foreign policy reacted to world changes by expanding overseas, then entering World War I, and finally hesitating about permanent leagues. Continuity stayed in commercial ambition and Caribbean watchfulness. Change showed in formal empire after the Spanish-American War when the Maine crisis and Treaty of Paris brought new island possessions. Mahan influenced naval building, Open Door talk sought Chinese markets, and the Roosevelt Corollary plus the Panama Canal tightened regional power. Dollar diplomacy added loans as tools beside troops. Anti-imperialists objected but did not reverse the gains. When Europe collapsed into war, neutrality gave way to intervention after submarine warfare and the Zimmermann crisis, then Wilson's League plan failed in the Senate so the 1920s preferred limited agreements over deep alliance. Policy kept answering external shocks rather than returning to pure isolation, even when leaders spoke carefully about entanglement and voters wanted distance from Europe's next quarrels. Across the whole span, reaction replaced any single lasting doctrine of isolation or empire. Across the whole span, reaction replaced any single lasting doctrine of isolation or empire. Across the whole span, reaction replaced any single lasting doctrine of isolation or empire.
